body:
PARKS, RECREATION AND CULTURE COMMITTEE 
M I N U T E S 
 
Wednesday, February 25, 2026, 5:00 pm 
Council Chamber, City Hall 
4949 Canada Way, Burnaby, BC 
 
PRESENT: 
Councillor Daniel Tetrault, Chair 
Councillor James Wang, Vice Chair 
Leaf Alifu, Resident Representative  
Gary Cheng, Resident Representative (participated electronically) 
Meg Holden, Resident Representative 
Amit Plaha, Resident Representative 
ADVISORY 
Trustee Kristin Schnider, School Board Representative 
MEMBERS 
Chris Peters, Tourism Burnaby 
ABSENT: 
Hillary Bergshoeff, Resident Representative 
Robin Dhir, Resident Representative 
STAFF: 
Mary Morrison-Clark, General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture 
Carmen Gonzalez, Deputy General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture 
Emmaline Hill, Director Culture 
Brad Domaas, Director Parks 
Andre Isakov, Director PRC Planning 
Judy Hamanishi, Director Recreation 
Rebecca Thandi, Director Strategic Initiatives 
Heather Edwards, Manager Parks Planning Design and Development 
Ian Wasson, Senior Current Planner 
Kathryn Matts, Administrative Officer 2 
 
1. 
CALL TO ORDER 
The Chair called the Committee meeting to order at 5:02 pm. 
*Meg Holden arrived at 5:03 pm 
  
2. 
TERRITORIAL ACKNOWLEDGEMENT 

 Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee Meeting 
Minutes February 25, 2026 
 
2 
The Chair, Councillor Daniel Tetrault, respectfully acknowledged that the City of 
Burnaby is located on the unceded territories of the xʷməθkʷəy̓ əm (Musqueam), 
Sḵwx̱ wú7mesh (Squamish), səlilwətaɬ (Tsleil-Waututh), and kʷikʷəƛ̓ əm (Kwikwetlem) 
Peoples. Each Nation has distinct histories and distinct traditional territories that fully or 
partially encompass the city. The City encourages you to learn more about the Host 
Nations whose ancestors have occupied and used these lands, including parts of 
present-day Burnaby, for thousands of years. 
 
3. 
ADOPTION OF AGENDA 
3.1 
Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee Agenda for February 25, 2026 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the agenda for the Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee meeting of 
Wednesday, February 25, 2026 be adopted, as presented. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
4. 
ADOPTION OF MINUTES 
4.1 
Minutes of the Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee meeting held on 
January 28, 2026 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the minutes of the Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee meeting held 
on January 28, 2026, be now adopted. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
5. 
DELEGATIONS AND INVITED PRESENTATIONS 
 
There were no Delegations or Invited Presentations. 
 
6. 
ADMINISTRATIVE REPORTS 
6.1 
DRAFT PRC PLAN 
The General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture submitted a report to 
provide information and seek feedback on the draft Making Life Better: Parks, 
Recreation and Culture Plan. 
*Gary Cheng arrived at 5:15 pm 

 Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee Meeting 
Minutes February 25, 2026 
 
3 
John Musil, Manager PRC Policy and Rebecca Anderson, Research Officer, 
Parks and Recreation, provided a presentation summarizing the report and 
advised of the feedback received at the Committee of the Whole (COTW) 
meeting held on February 24, 2026, where the COTW requested reprioritizing 
“Action #20” to earlier delivery. 
The Committee agreed with the change and staff undertook to update 
Attachment 1 prior to submission to Council.  
The Committee noted the importance of identifying partner groups involved when 
mentioning collaboration in the Plan. Staff undertook to have that information 
added to the supporting documents where applicable. 
The Committee further noted the importance of connecting the public 
involvement to the Plan and creating a space for the value of what residents can 
bring to the plan through volunteering opportunities. 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the draft Making Life Better: Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan as 
provided in Attachment 1 of the report, “Draft PRC Plan”, dated February 25, 
2026, be approved; and 
THAT staff be authorized to proceed with the second round of public engagement 
as outlined in Section 4.0 of this report.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
7. 
INFORMATION REPORTS 
7.1 
OPENING NEW RECREATION FACILITIES: PROJECT PLAN-PEOPLE-PLAY 
The General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture submitted a report 
providing information on the Project Plan-People-Play. 
 
 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the report titled “Opening New Recreation Facilities: Project Plan-People-
Play”, dated February 25, 2026, be received for information. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
8. 
CORRESPONDENCE 
 
There was no correspondence received. 
 
9. 
OTHER BUSINESS 

 Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee Meeting 
Minutes February 25, 2026 
 
4 
9.1 
Tourism Burnaby - 2028 Canadian Urban Forest Conference 
Chris Peters, Advisory member of the Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee 
representing Tourism Burnaby, provided information on the 2028 Canadian 
Urban Forest Conference (CUFC) and noted the Conference is a biennial 
national event that brings together approximately 350 urban forestry 
professionals, municipal leaders, researchers and industry experts. C. Peters 
advised the core focus of the Conference is urban greening, climate resilience, 
sustainability strategy, and community health impacts. 
C. Peters advised that Tourism Burnaby is asking for support in principle from the 
Committee to allow for the pursuit of a bid to host the 2028 CUFC. The support in 
principle would allow Tourism Burnaby to submit a proposal by the May deadline, 
as well as have staff report back outlining the anticipated staff time, planning 
considerations, and resource implications required and would not include a 
request for financial contribution from the City. 
 
Arising from discussion, the Committee expressed unanimous support for the 
request and introduced the following motion: 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT Tourism Burnaby’s pursuit of a bid to host the 2028 Canadian Urban 
Forest Conference, as discussed at the February 25, 2026, Parks, Recreation 
and Culture Committee meeting, be supported in principle. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
10. 
ADJOURNMENT 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee adjourn at 6:10 pm. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Councillor Daniel Tetrault 
CHAIR 
 
Kathryn Matts 
ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER 2

---

Speakers: Tyler Martin, Vice President and Brennan Bastyovanzsky, Bid Co-Chair

Celebration.

---

Presenters : Judi Ambrozaitis-Torrao - Aquatic Leader, Anastasia Bocolas -  Lead Hand Eco-Sculpture, and Tatiana Spysznyk - Display Technician

---

Purpose: To provide an update and seek direction on the Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management plan. Staff presentation by Brad Domaas, Director Parks and Heather Edwards, Manager Parks, Planning, Design and Development

Meeting April 22, 2026 
File: 68000-01 
COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
PARKS, RECREATION AND CULTURE COMMITTEE (PRCC) 
 
FROM: 
DEPUTY GENERAL MANAGER PARKS, RECREATION AND 
CULTURE  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
DRAFT BURNABY MOUNTAIN TRAIL MANAGEMENT PLAN 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide an update and seek direction on the Draft Burnaby 
Mountain Trail Management Plan.  
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan, as outlined in the 
report titled “Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan” dated April 22, 
2026, be approved; and  
THAT staff be directed to proceed to the final round (Phase 4) of community 
engagement. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
The Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan (BMTMP) provides a practical, long-term 
framework to guide the planning, maintenance, restoration, and enhancement of the 
trail network in Burnaby Mountain Park over the next 15 years.  
Informed by a comprehensive assessment of trail conditions and user demand, the plan 
focuses on day-to-day operations, maintenance needs, and future capital 
improvements. Required resources have been identified to support informed decisions 
on asset management, service levels, and operational priorities.  
This report outlines the need for the plan, summarizes work completed to date, and 
presents the emerging draft strategy. It seeks council approval to proceed with the final-
phase public engagement to refine and finalize the BMTMP.  
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
Advancement of the Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan aligns with the following 
council-adopted policies, plans, and strategies: Management Plan for Burnaby Mountain 
Conservation Area (2000); Environmentally Sensitive Areas Strategy (1992); Burnaby 
Urban Forest Strategy (2025); Climate Action Framework (2019); and the Official 
Community Plan Draft (2025).  
2.0 BACKGROUND 

Subject: Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan 
Open PRCC Meeting April 22, 2026 Page 2 
 
 
Burnaby Mountain Park is a vibrant 590-acre natural park in the heart of BC’s lower 
mainland. The ocean views, mountain vistas, and 35km of recreational trails through 
forest landscapes attract thousands of visitors each year. Burnaby has managed the 
conservation area for over 30 years. During that time, significant population growth has 
increased pressure on the trail system as park visitation continues to rise.  
Many trail segments were constructed decades ago and were not designed to 
accommodate current levels of use. Recent inspections and condition assessments 
have identified capacity constraints, safety concerns, and environmental impacts.  
It is recognized that a review is required to gauge the level of service the community 
values and develop a program to align future actions and resource expenditures to meet 
that need.  
The BMTMP is a practical vision designed to balance interests and align efforts across 
user groups and city staff with a shared commitment to conservation and recreation 
management in the Conservation area. During extensive public engagement PRC staff 
have collaborated with residents, Council, and community organizations to assess 
strengths and opportunities for improvement.  
Achieving the plan’s objectives will require updated trail standards, continued system 
inspections, and a clear distinction between routine maintenance and capital 
reconstruction. The BMTMP establishes a prioritization framework to guide investment 
decisions based on site conditions, environmental sensitivity, and community needs, 
aligned with the city’s capital and operating budget processes.  
The plan also recognizes the interest expressed by the community in being involved in 
volunteer work that supports and sustains the valued resources. To reach the vision of 
the plan both staff and volunteers will play important roles.  
The plan is intended to remain both practical and flexible to enable the city to maintain 
and reconstruct select areas of the current trail network systematically over a 15-year 
period.  
Through development of the BMTMP, the importance of this unique natural resource 
amid a growing city and region has been confirmed. The Community and council have 
helped to guide a plan to support the long-term sustainment of recreational amenities 
through the incredible mountain landscapes over time. 
 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
The BMTMP recommends a 15-year Action Plan to maintain, restore and rebuild 
approximately 35 km of trails to current standards. The plan is intended to adapt over 
time through monitoring, staff collaboration, and regular updates.  
The BMTMP is being developed through a structured four phase process: 

Subject: Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan 
Open PRCC Meeting April 22, 2026 Page 3 
 
 
Phase 1: Key Findings (Complete) 
Delivered to Council on Apr. 2024 and PRCC on Sept.19th, 2024, this report provided a 
comprehensive assessment of trail conditions, user demand, and current maintenance 
practices.  
Phase 2: Visioning (Complete) 
Delivered to Council on July 28, 2025, this report established guiding principles and 
priorities aligned with conservation objectives and available city resources.  
Phase 3: Drafting the Strategy (We are here) 
This report includes an actionable draft strategy informed by the public, staff, and 
council.  
Phase 4: (Coming (Next) 
This report will incorporate further feedback from the public, staff, and the PRCC to 
finalize the BMTMP prior to council consideration.  
The draft BMTMP (Attachment 1) is organized around the vision that sets the direction 
for the entire plan. All the elements were informed by the Key Findings reports and 
What We Heard reports - attached as appendices in the draft plan.  
With Council’s approval, staff will move to phase 4 and seek additional community input 
in spring 2026 before presenting the final Strategy.  
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Clear communication and meaningful engagement are essential to building community 
support for the BMTMP. Feedback received so far has shown strong public 
endorsement for the Strategy, and we are committed to maintaining this positive 
dialogue. 
To support the next phase, staff will undertake an additional round of public engagement 
in early 2026. The draft strategy and an accompanying online survey will be posted on 
the city’s website. Feedback will help confirm community support, identify refinements, 
and ensure that key issues and opportunities have been considered. 
Beyond the public survey, the city will conduct targeted outreach to Burnaby’s 
stewardship community and Host Nations, ensuring that these key community partners 
are actively involved in the process.  
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The expected cost to implement the actions identified in the BMTMP is approximately 
$10 million over the 15-year period. Funding for implementation will be brought forward 
through the annual budget process for council consideration.  
 
 

Subject: Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan 
Open PRCC Meeting April 22, 2026 Page 4 
 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
Carmen Gonzalez, Deputy General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture 
ATTACHMENTS 
Attachment 1 – Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan 
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS 
This report was prepared by Heather Edwards, Manager PRC Planning and reviewed 
by Andre Isakov, Director PRC Planning, and Brad Domas, Director Parks Operations.

---

Purpose: To provide an update on the IDEAs in Public Recreation research project, led by Langara College, outlining its purpose, partnership model, and progress to date, while sharing emerging insights and demonstrating how the work supports improved access, inclusion, and participation in public recreation for Burnaby’s diverse communities. Presentation by Yue-Ching Cheng, Department Chair and Research Coordinator, Langara College

Meeting April 22, 2026 
File: 54000-00 
COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
PARKS, RECREATION AND CULTURE COMMITTEE (PRCC) 
 
FROM: 
DEPUTY GENERAL MANAGER PARKS, RECREATION AND 
CULTURE  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
INCLUSION, DIVERSITY, EQUITY AND ACCESSIBILITY (IDEAS) IN 
PUBLIC RECREATION RESEARCH IN BURNABY  
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide an update on the IDEAs in Public Recreation research 
project, led by Langara College, outlining its purpose, partnership 
model, and progress to date, while sharing emerging insights and 
demonstrating how the work supports improved access, inclusion, and 
participation in public recreation for Burnaby’s diverse communities. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Inclusion, Diversity, Equity and Accessibility (IDEAS) in 
Public Recreation Research in Burnaby” dated April 22, 2026, be received for 
information. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This report provides an update on the IDEAs in Public Recreation research project, led 
by Langara College, including its purpose, partnership model, and progress to date. 
Using the 5As of Access and Belonging, Dignity, and Justice frameworks, the project 
examines how immigrant and racialized communities experience public recreation, 
alongside how services are designed and delivered. Emerging insights highlight key 
barriers to participation and opportunities to strengthen access, inclusion, and belong in 
Burnaby’s recreation system. The report also outlines next steps to validate findings 
with partners and translate them into practical actions. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
This Report aligns with the Benefits Based Approach (2024). 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Project Overview 
The IDEAs (Inclusion, Diversity, Equity, and Accessibility) in Public Recreation project is 
a multi-year applied research initiative led by Langara College in partnership with 
Burnaby’s Parks, Recreation and Culture (PRC) department and community 
organizations. The project is being implemented across multiple municipal contexts in 

Subject: Inclusion, Diversity, Equity and Accessibility (IDEA) in Public Recreation Research in Burnaby  
Open PRCC Meeting April 22, 2026 Page 2 
 
British Columbia, including the Comox Valley, allowing for comparison across different 
community settings. 
The project explores how immigrant and racialized communities understand, access, 
and experience public recreation, alongside how recreation staff understand inclusion 
and deliver services within existing systems. 
Two complementary frameworks guide this work: 
• 
The 5As of Access, which examine key conditions that shape participation:  
o Availability – whether programs and services exist (physical and/or 
perceptual) 
o Accessibility – whether people can physically and practically access 
them.  
o Adequacy – whether services meet the needs of diverse communities.  
o Agency – whether individuals feel they have the ability to decide on 
recreation opportunities and shape the design of them. 
o Acceptability – whether programs feel relevant, welcoming, and 
appropriate.  
• 
Belonging, Dignity, and Justice (BDJJ), which focuses on whether individuals 
feel welcomed, respected, and meaningfully included.  
Together, these frameworks allow the project to move beyond access alone, toward a 
more complete understanding of participation in recreation. 
This work is supported through a federally funded College and Community Social 
Innovation Fund (CCSIF) grant, which supports applied research partnerships between 
post-secondary institutions and community organizations to address real-world 
challenges.  
Partnership Approach 
A core feature of the project is its collaborative design. The research is conducted in 
partnership with: 
 
• 
British Columbia Recreation & Parks Association (BCRPA) 
• 
Community-based organizations serve newcomers and racialized populations. 
• 
Municipal recreation departments  
 
Partners have been involved throughout the process and are supporting community 
engagement. 
 

Subject: Inclusion, Diversity, Equity and Accessibility (IDEA) in Public Recreation Research in Burnaby  
Open PRCC Meeting April 22, 2026 Page 3 
 
 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
Progress to date  
The project has now completed its primary data collection phase across both 
community members and recreation staff.  
3.1 Municipal Staff Survey 
• 
A survey and interviews with Burnaby recreation staff explored:  
o how staff understand and support the 5As in practice  
o perceived barriers to participation  
o current practices and constraints  
o confidence in engaging in diverse communities.  
3.1.1 Framework-Informed Design 
• 
Both community and staff tools were intentionally designed using the 5As and 
BDJJ frameworks to capture both structural conditions and lived experience.  
The project is now moving into the validation phase, where findings will be reviewed 
with partners and community to ensure they reflect both community perspectives and 
operational realities. 
3.1.2 Emerging Insights 
Preliminary analysis across both community and staff data highlights several consistent 
themes: 
• 
Availability Does Not Ensure Participation 
Even where programs exist, participation is influenced by accessibility and 
adequacy. Information availability is a frequently cited barrier.  
• 
Different Understandings of Recreation 
The concept of “recreation” is not universal and does not always align with how 
services are currently defined or promoted, affecting awareness and 
acceptability.  
• 
Belonging Drives Ongoing Engagement 
Participants feel welcome in recreation spaces and by staff, but for some, not all 
activities feel comfortable to participate in due to lack of familiarity. 
• 
Gaps Between Community Experience and System Design 
Early findings suggest differences between how services are intended and how 
diverse communities experience them. Cultural norms have also become known 

Subject: Inclusion, Diversity, Equity and Accessibility (IDEA) in Public Recreation Research in Burnaby  
Open PRCC Meeting April 22, 2026 Page 4 
 
in how groups experience recreation services (collective participation vs 
individual participation in recreation).  
• 
Staff Commitment and System Constraints 
Staff demonstrate a strong commitment to inclusive practice, alongside 
recognition of operational constraints that can limit responsiveness. 
3.1.2 Next Steps 
The next phase will focus on validating and mobilizing findings: 
• 
Validation sessions with community partners  
• 
Collaborative interpretation to identify practical actions.  
• 
Development of training for recreation staff 
• 
Sharing findings across the recreation sector  
• 
Development of co-design framework 
 
Upon receipt of the final research findings and report, targeted training informed by 
the results will be developed. This training will support increased participation of 
racialized and immigrant residents in recreation programs by addressing identified 
barriers and advancing inclusive service delivery. 
Relevance to Burnaby 
This work is directly relevant to ongoing efforts to: 
• 
Strengthen access across all dimensions of the 5As.  
• 
Improve how services reflect and respond to diverse community needs.  
• 
Support staff in delivering inclusive and responsive programs.  
• 
Enhance community belonging and participation.  
• 
Inform of evidence-based planning and decision-making.  
 
The findings may also support broader connections across sectors, including 
community services and education, where inclusive recreation contributes to overall 
community wellbeing. 
Conclusion 
The IDEAs in Public Recreation project are entering a phase where community and 
staff-informed insights will be validated and translated into practical applications. By 
combining a focus on access (5As) with lived experience (BDJJ), this work supports a 
more comprehensive approach to building recreation systems that are inclusive, 
responsive, and meaningful for all community members. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 

Subject: Inclusion, Diversity, Equity and Accessibility (IDEA) in Public Recreation Research in Burnaby  
Open PRCC Meeting April 22, 2026 Page 5 
 
• 
Community survey and engagement data captured how residents experience 
recreation across the 5As.  
Focus groups and interviews were conducted with racialized and immigrant 
groups to further explore their experiences from a BDJJ perspective. The focus 
was on new immigrants identified by project partners (Burnaby Neighborhood 
House and MOASIC BC). 
 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The City of Burnaby’s contributions to the project are primarily in-kind. This includes 
staff time for surveys, interviews, EDI training, and participation in the co-creation 
process, as well as the provision of local knowledge and information. Facility space will 
also be provided at no charge to support in-person meetings and workshops.  
Respectfully submitted, 
 
Mary Morrison-Clark, General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture 
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS 
This report was prepared by Rebecca Thandi, Director Strategic Initiatives, PRC and 
reviewed by Carmen Gonzalez Deputy General Manager PRC.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan Approval and Final Public Engagement
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee is set to vote on approving a Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan — a 15-year blueprint for maintaining, restoring, and rebuilding the park's 35 km trail network. If approved, staff would move forward with a final round of public engagement before the plan is presented to full Council for a binding decision.
briefs.action_required: Visit burnaby.ca/BurnabyMountainTrails to review the draft plan and watch for the upcoming public survey.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan — PRCC approval vote meeting date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Natural Resources, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Parks Plan Advances and Urban Forest Conference Bid Supported
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee has moved two items forward to full Council for consideration. A draft long-term parks and recreation plan called 'Making Life Better' is set to go to a second round of public engagement, meaning residents and businesses will have another chance to give feedback. The Committee also gave in-principle support — a non-binding show of support — for Tourism Burnaby to bid on hosting the 2028 Canadian Urban Forest Conference, a national event focused on urban greening and climate resilience.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the public engagement period on Burnaby's draft Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan — your input can shape it.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Roques Rugby Delegation Celebrates Bingham Cup Bid
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to hear a delegation from Roques Rugby, represented by Vice President Tyler Martin and Bid Co-Chair Brennan Bastyovanzsky, in celebration of their Bingham Cup bid. The Bingham Cup is an international gay rugby tournament. No business regulation or bylaw changes appear to be proposed at this time.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Presentation on Engaging New Adults in Recreation Through Play
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to hear an invited presentation from three City recreation and arts staff members on how to get adults who are new to recreation more involved through play-based activities. This is an informational presentation — no bylaw or policy change is being voted on at this time — but it may shape future recreation programming decisions by the City.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan and Final Public Engagement
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee is set to vote on approving a draft 15-year plan to maintain, restore, and upgrade the 35 km of trails on Burnaby Mountain. If approved, the plan would cost roughly $10 million over 15 years and staff would move forward with one final round of public consultation before the plan is finalized.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the public engagement survey in spring 2026 and submit your feedback before it closes.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan — PRCC vote on draft approval
Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan — Phase 4 public engagement opens (spring 2026)

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Research Update on Inclusion in Burnaby Public Recreation Programs
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee is set to receive an update on a multi-year research project led by Langara College. The project studies how immigrant and racialized communities experience public recreation in Burnaby, and what barriers stop them from taking part — with the goal of making recreation programs more welcoming and accessible for everyone.
briefs.action_required: Watch for findings — future staff training and program changes may affect recreation-related businesses.
